title: Exponential speedups in fault-tolerant processing of quantum experiments
authors: Ishaan Kannan, Harald Putterman, Jordan Cotler
abstract: Quantum information processing has the potential to substantially enhance how we learn from physical experiments, but coupling a quantum processor to an experimental sample introduces noise that can exponentially degrade learning even when the processor itself is fault-tolerant. In this work, we show that fault tolerance can nevertheless be leveraged to recover exponential speedups by embedding the unknown system into an arbitrarily high-distance quantum code with only constant error overhead and running a fault-tolerant learning algorithm. Using this $\textit{quantum uploading}$ procedure, we prove that both classical shadow tomography and the estimation of cubic observables can be performed exponentially faster than by any adaptive strategy that does not immediately upload the state into encoded memory. These separations hold even when the uploading stage is substantially noisier than the bare experimental interface. To prove them, we introduce the Heisenberg learning tree method, a flexible tool for obtaining learning lower bounds when the limited resource is not quantum replicas but an experimentally motivated constraint such as noise. We numerically illustrate the speedups in an astronomical imaging application, where quantum processing of individual uploaded photons locates an exoplanet obscured by a bright star using orders of magnitude fewer shots than unencoded baselines. Our results establish fault-tolerant quantum computation as a valuable tool for learning from quantum experiments.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the exponential speedups of quantum learning can be preserved despite noisy experimental interfaces by using fault-tolerant quantum error correction to 'upload' states into encoded memory. The authors prove that this method outperforms adaptive classical strategies for tasks like shadow tomography and demonstrate its utility in a simulated astronomical imaging task.
main_problem: The noise introduced when coupling a quantum processor to an experimental sample can exponentially degrade the efficiency of quantum learning, even if the processor itself is fault-tolerant.
main_result: By using a 'quantum uploading' procedure to embed unknown systems into high-distance quantum codes, exponential speedups in tasks like classical shadow tomography and cubic observable estimation can be recovered, even with noisy uploading stages.
method: The authors introduce the 'Heisenberg learning tree method' to derive learning lower bounds under noise constraints and propose a quantum uploading procedure into encoded memory.
model_or_system: The paper discusses a general framework for learning from quantum experiments, specifically illustrating the application to astronomical imaging of photons to locate an exoplanet.
key_observables: Classical shadow tomography and the estimation of cubic observables.
important_parameters: Quantum code distance, noise levels in the uploading stage, and the number of experimental shots.
main_assumptions: The efficiency gains assume the ability to embed the system into an arbitrarily high-distance quantum code with constant error overhead.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of noise in quantum-experimental coupling, proposes the quantum uploading method, develops the Heisenberg learning tree method for lower bounds, and provides a numerical illustration using an astronomical imaging application.
why_interesting: It addresses a critical bottleneck in quantum sensing and measurement—the interface between physical samples and quantum processors—and provides a theoretical pathway to maintain quantum advantages in noisy, real-world experimental settings.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
